entry.title: SAP Sellers & SIs: Where Deals Slip and Untracked Execution Risk
entry.content:
Something I’ve noticed working with SAP system integrators in regulated markets: Deals don’t usually slip at procurement because of relationships or pricing.

They slip because of untracked execution risk.

Especially in complex enterprise deals where sales, delivery, and compliance all intersect.

A few observations:

  • A compliance or procurement certificate lapses weeks before submission
  • A regulatory change (tax, reporting, or industry-specific requirement) wasn’t factored into the solution
  • A board-level or ministry stakeholder with approval authority was never engaged
  • A localisation gap (discovered late in S/4 or integration scope) surfaces after signature

None of these are big issues on their own and yes, strong teams should catch them.

But across long-cycle SAP deals with multiple stakeholders, systems, and regulatory layers, it’s not always that no one is watching, it’s that there’s too much to track across too many moving parts. 

Does anyone else feel this pain?

I’ve been speaking with SAP practice leads and enterprise sellers navigating this across regions, especially in regulated industries like finance, oil and gas, and pharma.

Curious to hear from others here: Where have you seen deals slip late in the process and what was the root cause?

entry.title: Variable inheritance issue between classic and optimized stories when opening via links, hyperlinks
entry.content:
Problem Description
In the source story, there is a KPI table with links to other stories.
The behavior when opening these links differs depending on:

The type of target story (classic vs optimized)
Whether the link opens in the same tab or a new tab
The target story configuration (saved or server values)

 

1. Classic Story

When opening the link, the selected variables (Mes/Año and Programa) are correctly inherited in the target story.
The variable prompt appears pre-filled with values from the source story.

2. Optimized Story

If the target story is configured to use server-defined values, it does not inherit the current variables.
If configured to use current variable selections, it works correctly.
Even when adding parameters in the URL (vXXPar, vXXVal or fXXModel, fXXDim, fXXVal), the variable prompt remains empty.

3. Opening Method

Script onClick with NavigationUtils.openStory:
Same tab: sometimes variables are inherited
New tab: variable prompt is empty.
Hyperlink in table or widget:
Same tab: similar behavior to script.
New tab: variable prompt is empty, values not inherited.

entry.title: Re: SAP Note 2743317 Does not work
entry.content:
SAP Note 2743317 provides guidance for missing authorizations, but it doesn’t cover every backend scenario. The object QFGEN is tied to Quality Management (QM) Fiori apps and requires specific authorization objects (e.g., S_SERVICE, S_RFC, S_USER_GRP). If your system has custom roles or has not been updated with the latest support package, the note alone won’t resolve the issue.

Having said that, the recommended next actions are

  • Run SU53 immediately after the error to pinpoint missing authorizations.
  • Compare your user’s role with the SAP standard business role for the app.
  • If still unresolved, raise an OSS message to SAP referencing Note 2743317 and include your SU53 trace.

entry.title: Re: SAP GUI UPGRADE
entry.content:
Hello customer,

Thank you for your question.

SAP always recommend to upgrade the SAP GUI to the latest available patch level in:

The latest SAP GUI version and patch can be downloaded from the SAP for Me Support Portal:

Software Center - SAP for Me

# "Installations & Upgrades"

 # "By Category"

  # "SAP Frontend Components"

   # "SAP GUI FOR WINDOWS"

    # "SAP GUI FOR WINDOWS 8.00 CORE "

     # "Support Packages and Patches"

        # SAP GUI for Windows 8.00 PL16 (Note 1053737) ----> always check this note for updates regarding patch releases.

 

The important changes and improvements in SAP GUI 8.00 are outlined in the SAP Note 3075781 - New and changed features in SAP GUI for Windows 8.00.

The most relevant change and improvements refers to the 64bit version availability: 

True 64bit version of SAP GUI for Windows

Up to release 7.70, SAP GUI for Windows was only available as a 32bit version with the exception being the 64bit versions of the automation controls (Logon Control, Table Factory Control, BAPI Control and Function Control). The 32bit version works well on 64bit operating systems and runs in the so-called “emulation” (see also https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/win32/winprog64/running-32-bit-applications). However, this has a couple of drawbacks when it comes to memory utilization and integration with other 64bit processes. The most important limitation in this area was overcome when we started supporting 64bit Microsoft Office versions in SAP Desktop Office Integration.

Regardless of this, we keep getting requests for full 64bit support in SAP GUI for Windows and thus decided to implement, deliver, and support such a version. Therefore, as of SAP GUI for Windows 8.00, the SAP GUI delivery includes both a 32bit version and a 64bit version. You can decide which version to use (parallel usage of both versions on the same client PC is not possible).
The 64bit version offers an almost complete feature set with some differences to the 32bit version which are documented in SAP Note 3218166 - "SAP GUI for Windows: Functional differences of the 64bit version compared to the 32bit version".

Although there are some restrictions with 64bit version, in case those restrictions outlined SAP Note 3218166 are not relevant within your SAP landscape scenario, we  recommend to upgrade to this bit version due to memory utilization and compatibility with other 64-bit external softwares. 

 

Best Regards,

SAP Support

entry.title: Re: Issue with Location-Level Restriction Not Working
entry.content:
Run STAUTHTRACE for the affected user while they post in MIGO. If you are not the part of Security team, ask them to execute this transaction.  This will show if the system is checking M_MSEG_LGO or any other storage location–related objects. If the check is not happening, it means the restriction is not active in your Public Cloud environment.

Check Business Role Restrictions in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud. Unlike on-premise, Public Cloud uses business role restrictions (not just authorization objects). Navigate to Maintain Business Roles → Restrictions → Storage Location and configure allowed values. Block Storage Location in Customizing (if available in your edition). Some Public Cloud tenants allow blocking storage locations for postings. This ensures no entries can be made, regardless of role.

entry.title: Re: add new tax classification sap public cloud
entry.content: You cannot create new tax classification codes in SAP Public Cloud yourself. They are SAP-delivered and maintained via CBC. If you need a new one, you must request it from SAP.

entry.title: Re: ZERO base price requirement in sales order
entry.content: For descriptive repair items, use TATX text items directly in the sales order. For materials that must exist in master data, assign SERV material type and maintain PPR0 with 0 value. If neither works, raise an SAP support ticket to confirm if a fit-to-standard workaround exists in Public Cloud for repair processes.

entry.title: Digital signature in Adobe Forms
entry.content:
Hello, all.

I am trying to use digital signatures with Adobe Forms.

I have created an Adobe Form that works correctly and is generated/printed without any issues.

I have now added a new Digital Signature field, and it is located in the following position in the hierarchy:

This is the code I'm using after the FP_JOB_CLOSE:

    DATA: lo_fp     TYPE REF TO if_fp,
          lo_pdfobj TYPE REF TO if_fp_pdf_object,
          lv_signed TYPE xstring,
          lv_alias  TYPE string,
          lv_field  TYPE string.

*    lv_field = 'data.Firma_Digital'.
*    lv_field = 'Firma_Digital'.
    lv_field = 'data.pagina_principal.Firma_Digital'.
*    lv_field = 'data.Firma_Digital[0]'.

    TRY.
        lo_fp     = cl_fp=>get_reference( ).
        lo_pdfobj = lo_fp->create_pdf_object(
                      connection = 'ADS_SSL' ). "ls_fp_outputparams-connection ). "cl_fp=>get_ads_connection( )

*lo_pdfobj->get_pdf_fields(

        IF iv_firma EQ c_tipo_firma-dir_economico.
          lv_alias = 'CredencialDirectorFinancer'.

          lv_alias = 'CredencialApoderat'.
        ENDIF.

***      Test
**        lo_pdfobj->set_template(
**          xftdata  = ls_fp_formoutput-pdf
**          fillable = abap_true ).

        " Indicar alias y campo
        lo_pdfobj->set_signature(
          keyname   = lv_alias
          fieldname = lv_field ).

        " Ejecutar la firma
        lo_pdfobj->execute( ).

        " Recuperar PDF firmado
        lo_pdfobj->get_document( IMPORTING pdfdata = lv_signed ).

        ev_pdf = lv_signed.

      CATCH cx_fp_runtime_usage INTO DATA(lx_usage).
        ev_mensaje = lx_usage->get_longtext( ).
        IF ev_mensaje IS INITIAL.
          ev_mensaje = lx_usage->errmsgshort.
        ENDIF.
        RETURN.
      CATCH cx_fp_runtime_system INTO DATA(lx_system).
        ev_mensaje = lx_system->get_longtext( ).
        IF ev_mensaje IS INITIAL.
          ev_mensaje = lx_system->errmsgshort.
        ENDIF.
        RETURN.

        ev_mensaje = lx_internal->get_longtext( ).
        IF ev_mensaje IS INITIAL.
          ev_mensaje = lx_internal->errmsgshort.

I've always got the exception cx_fp_runtime_system  in the statement lo_pdfobj->execute( )  with text:

com.adobe.ProcessingException: com.adobe.livecycle.signatures.client.types.exceptions.MissingSignatureFieldException: ALC-DSS-302-009 The specified Signature Field data.pagina_principal.Firma_Digital does not exist (in the operation : getSignatureField)

I've tried to move the Singnature field to other part of the form, but have the same problem... I've tried also to debug the method  execute() but couldn't find the error.

Thanks in advance.

entry.title: Master Data and Price Procedure from Sales order to invoice
entry.content:
Hey everyone,

I have a question about the OTC flow (Sales Order → Delivery → Invoice) and how pricing behaves when copy control is set to not re-determine prices during invoice creation.

In this setup, the system simply copies the pricing data from the sales order — even if some master data changes between the delivery and invoice steps, including condition types that depend on that master data.

One workaround I'm thinking of: manually update the master data on the sales order and recalculate pricing (VA02), then update the relevant field on the delivery (VL02N) if it exists there — and then create a fresh invoice with the updated data.

My question is: if the delivery is already completed/goods issued, can doing this process cause any issues or impact in other steps of the flow?

Thanks in advance!

entry.title: Re: BAPI_SAG_CHANGE Insert Pricing Condition
entry.content: You cannot insert new pricing conditions with BAPI_SAG_CHANGE alone. Use the dedicated condition BAPIs (or OData APIs in Public Cloud) to create/update conditions, and use BAPI_SAG_CHANGE only for item/schedule changes.

entry.title: SAP Agentic AI in Practice: Building the SAP Exception Log Triage Agent (Part 3 of 6)
entry.content:
SAP COMMUNITY  |  SERIES 2: SAP AGENTIC AI IN PRACTICE  |  PART 3 OF 6

SAP Agentic AI in Practice: Building the SAP Exception Log Triage Agent

From reactive triage to proactive monitoring — a multi-agent system with a router that delegates SM21 system log errors and SM37 job failures to specialized sub-agents, correlates findings across log types, and escalates autonomously.

By Deepa Dorairaj   |  SAP AI Solution Architect  |  Series 2, Part 3 of 6  |  Published 2026

TL;DR: Part 3 introduces the multi-agent pattern — a router agent that classifies incoming SAP errors and delegates to specialized sub-agents: one for SM21 system log analysis, one for SM37 job failure triage. The router also identifies correlations across log types that neither sub-agent sees alone. The article covers two stages: Stage A reactive triage (triggered on demand) and Stage B proactive monitoring (continuous, schedule-driven). This is the architectural foundation for the multi-agent orchestration in Part 6.

Series Context

This is Part 3 of SAP Agentic AI in Practice. Parts 1 and 2 built single agents — a data quality assessor and a migration assessment agent with conditional routing. Part 3 introduces multi-agent coordination: a router agent that classifies errors and delegates to specialized sub-agents. This pattern is the core building block for Part 6, where all agents in this series work together under a unified orchestrator.

The exception log triage use case is ideal for introducing multi-agent patterns because SAP error landscapes involve genuinely different log types that require different analysis approaches. A single agent handling SM21 and SM37 in the same reasoning chain produces lower quality findings than two specialized agents, each focused on their domain, coordinated by a router that sees the full picture.

WHY MULTI-AGENT FOR SAP LOG TRIAGE 

Why Log Triage Benefits from Multi-Agent Architecture

SAP exception triage in production environments involves multiple log sources that are related but structurally different:

Log Type

What It Contains

Who Cares

SM21 System Log

Platform-level errors — work process failures, memory issues, RFC connection problems, kernel errors. Context is technical infrastructure.

SAP Basis / Platform team

SM37 Job Log

Business process failures — scheduled job errors, batch processing failures, data load problems. Context is business process continuity.

Functional / Operations team

Correlation layer

When an SM37 job fails because of an SM21 RFC connection error — neither log alone tells the full story. The router sees both.

Both teams need joint view

A single agent reasoning over both log types simultaneously tends to conflate the contexts — applying infrastructure reasoning to business process errors and vice versa. Specialized sub-agents with a coordinating router produce more accurate findings and clearer escalation paths.

MULTI-AGENT ARCHITECTURE OVERVIEW 

Router + Sub-Agent Architecture

Error batch input → Router agent classifies → SM21 sub-agent → Correlate findings → Severity score + escalation
		→	SM37 sub-agent	→

The router agent has three responsibilities:

  • Classify incoming errors — determine which log type each error belongs to
  • Delegate to the appropriate specialized sub-agent
  • Correlate findings across both sub-agents — identify cases where SM21 infrastructure errors are causing SM37 job failures

 

Agent

Responsibility

Router agent

Classifies errors, delegates to sub-agents, correlates cross-log findings, produces unified severity score

SM21 sub-agent

Analyzes system log errors — identifies platform issues, RFC failures, memory problems, kernel errors

SM37 sub-agent

Analyzes job log failures — identifies which jobs failed, why, business process impact, retry feasibility

STATE SCHEMA AND TOOLS 

State Schema and Tool Definitions

Triage State Schema
# triage_agent.py — multi-agent exception log triage
from typing import TypedDict, List, Optional, Dict
from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, END
 
class TriageState(TypedDict):
    raw_errors:         List[dict]   # Incoming error batch
    sm21_errors:        List[dict]   # Classified SM21 errors
    sm37_errors:        List[dict]   # Classified SM37 errors
    sm21_findings:      List[dict]   # SM21 sub-agent findings
    sm37_findings:      List[dict]   # SM37 sub-agent findings
    correlations:       List[dict]   # Cross-log correlations
    overall_severity:   str          # CRITICAL/HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW
    escalation_needed:  bool
    escalation_targets: List[str]    # Which teams to notify
    triage_report:      Optional[str]

Tool Definitions

# tools/triage_tools.py
from langchain_core.tools import tool
import hdbcli.dbapi as hdb, json, os
 
def get_conn():
    return hdb.connect(address=os.environ['HANA_HOST'], port=443,
        user=os.environ['HANA_USER'],
        password=os.environ['HANA_PASSWORD'], encrypt=True)
 
@tool
def get_sm21_errors(hours_back: int = 24) -> str:
    """Fetch recent SM21 system log errors from SAP HANA."""
    conn = get_conn()
    cursor = conn.cursor()
    cursor.execute(f'''
        SELECT
            MANDT, DATUM, UZEIT, MTYPE,
            MSGID, MSGNO, MSGTX,
            REPID, ABAP_RUNTIME_ERROR
        FROM SYSLOG
        WHERE DATUM >= ADD_DAYS(CURRENT_DATE, -{hours_back // 24})
        AND MTYPE IN ('E', 'A', 'X')
        -- E=Error, A=Abend, X=Exit
        ORDER BY DATUM DESC, UZEIT DESC
        LIMIT 200
    ''')
    errors = []
    for row in cursor.fetchall():
        errors.append({
            'date': str(row[1]), 'time': str(row[2]),
            'type': row[3], 'message_id': row[4],
            'message_text': row[6], 'program': row[7],
            'runtime_error': row[8]
        })
    return json.dumps({'sm21_errors': errors, 'count': len(errors)})
 
@tool
def get_sm37_failures(hours_back: int = 24) -> str:
    """Fetch recent SM37 job failures from SAP HANA."""
    conn = get_conn()
    cursor = conn.cursor()
    cursor.execute(f'''
        SELECT
            JOBNAME, JOBCOUNT, STATUS,
            SDLSTRTDT, SDLSTRTTM,
            LASTCHGDT, LASTCHGTM,
            JOBCLASS, PRGNAME
        FROM TBTCO
        WHERE STATUS = 'A'
        -- A = Aborted
        AND SDLSTRTDT >= ADD_DAYS(CURRENT_DATE, -{hours_back // 24})
        ORDER BY LASTCHGDT DESC, LASTCHGTM DESC
        LIMIT 100
    ''')
    failures = []
    for row in cursor.fetchall():
        failures.append({
            'job_name': row[0], 'job_count': row[1],
            'status': row[2], 'start_date': str(row[3]),
            'start_time': str(row[4]), 'job_class': row[7],
            'program': row[8]
        })
    return json.dumps({'sm37_failures': failures, 'count': len(failures)})
 
@tool
def get_job_log_details(job_name: str, job_count: str) -> str:
    """Get detailed log messages for a specific failed SM37 job."""
    conn = get_conn()
    cursor = conn.cursor()
    cursor.execute(f'''
        SELECT MSGTY, MSGID, MSGNO, MSGV1, MSGV2
        FROM TBTCP
        WHERE JOBNAME = '{job_name}'
        AND JOBCOUNT = '{job_count}'
        AND MSGTY IN ('E', 'A')
        ORDER BY LNSNR
    ''')
    messages = [{'type': r[0], 'msg_id': r[1],
                 'msg_no': r[2], 'text': f'{r[3]} {r[4]}'.strip()}
                for r in cursor.fetchall()]
    return json.dumps({'job': job_name, 'messages': messages})
STAGE A: REACTIVE MULTI-AGENT TRIAGE 

Stage A — Reactive Triage on Demand

Stage A is triggered manually or by an integration call — it receives a batch of errors, classifies them, runs sub-agents, and produces a triage report. This is the right pattern for on-call support workflows and integration with ITSM tools like ServiceNow.

Router Agent

# Router: classifies errors and delegates to sub-agents
from langgraph.prebuilt import create_react_agent
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI
 
llm = ChatOpenAI(proxy_model_name='gpt-4o')
 
router_prompt = '''You are a SAP exception log router agent.
Your job is to:
1. Fetch recent SM21 system log errors using get_sm21_errors
2. Fetch recent SM37 job failures using get_sm37_failures
3. Count and categorize errors by type and severity
4. Identify the top 3 most critical issues requiring immediate attention
5. Note any patterns suggesting SM21 errors are causing SM37 failures
6. Produce a structured classification summary for sub-agent processing

Be systematic. Fetch both log types before drawing conclusions.
Always explain your classification reasoning.'''
 
router_agent = create_react_agent(
    model=llm,
    tools=[get_sm21_errors, get_sm37_failures],
    state_modifier=router_prompt
)

SM21 Sub-Agent

# SM21 specialist: deep analysis of system log errors
sm21_prompt = '''You are an SAP Basis expert specializing in SM21 system logs.
Analyze the SM21 errors provided and for each significant error:

1. Identify the root cause (infrastructure, memory, RFC, kernel)
2. Assess business impact — which processes are affected
3. Classify severity: CRITICAL / HIGH / MEDIUM / LOW
4. Recommend immediate action
5. Recommend preventive measure

Focus on patterns — a single error may be noise,
ten of the same error in one hour is a systemic issue.
Always reference specific error message IDs in your findings.'''
 
sm21_agent = create_react_agent(
    model=llm,
    tools=[get_sm21_errors],
    state_modifier=sm21_prompt
)

SM37 Sub-Agent

# SM37 specialist: deep analysis of job failures
sm37_prompt = '''You are an SAP operations expert specializing in SM37 job failures.
For each failed job:

1. Use get_job_log_details to retrieve the specific failure messages
2. Identify the root cause (data issue, RFC failure, timeout, authorization)
3. Assess business process impact — what does this job do?
4. Classify severity based on job class and business criticality
5. Determine if the job can be safely restarted or requires fix-first
6. Identify dependent jobs that may be blocked

Job class A = highest priority. Always investigate class A failures first.'''
 
sm37_agent = create_react_agent(
    model=llm,
    tools=[get_sm37_failures, get_job_log_details],
    state_modifier=sm37_prompt
)
Stage A Orchestration — Router + Sub-Agents
# Stage A: Orchestrate router + sub-agents reactively
from langchain_core.messages import HumanMessage
import json
 
def run_reactive_triage(hours_back: int = 24) -> dict:
    """Run full reactive triage across SM21 and SM37 logs."""
 
    # Step 1: Router classifies and summarizes all errors
    router_result = router_agent.invoke({
        'messages': [HumanMessage(content=f'''
            Fetch and classify all SAP errors from the last {hours_back} hours.
            Summarize SM21 system errors and SM37 job failures separately.
            Flag any cases where system errors appear to be causing job failures.
        ''')]
    })
    router_summary = router_result['messages'][-1].content
 
    # Step 2: SM21 sub-agent performs deep analysis
    sm21_result = sm21_agent.invoke({
        'messages': [HumanMessage(content=f'''
            Perform deep analysis of SM21 system log errors from
            the last {hours_back} hours. Router summary for context:
            {router_summary}
        ''')]
    })
    sm21_findings = sm21_result['messages'][-1].content
 
    # Step 3: SM37 sub-agent performs deep analysis
    sm37_result = sm37_agent.invoke({
        'messages': [HumanMessage(content=f'''
            Perform deep analysis of SM37 job failures from
            the last {hours_back} hours. Get job log details for
            class A failures first. Router summary for context:
            {router_summary}
        ''')]
    })
    sm37_findings = sm37_result['messages'][-1].content
 
    # Step 4: Router correlates findings from both sub-agents
    correlation_result = router_agent.invoke({
        'messages': [HumanMessage(content=f'''
            Correlate these findings from both log types and produce
            a unified triage report with:
            1. Overall severity (CRITICAL/HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW)
            2. Top 3 issues requiring immediate action
            3. Cross-log correlations (SM21 errors causing SM37 failures)
            4. Escalation targets (Basis team / Operations team / Both)
            5. Recommended actions in priority order

            SM21 findings: {sm21_findings}
            SM37 findings: {sm37_findings}
        ''')]
    })
 
    return {
        'router_summary': router_summary,
        'sm21_findings': sm21_findings,
        'sm37_findings': sm37_findings,
        'triage_report': correlation_result['messages'][-1].content
    }
 
# Run reactive triage
result = run_reactive_triage(hours_back=24)
print(result['triage_report'])

Passing the router summary as context to each sub-agent is the key pattern here. The sub-agents don't operate in isolation — they receive the router's classification as context, which focuses their analysis on the most significant errors rather than treating every log entry with equal weight.

STAGE B: PROACTIVE MONITORING AGENT 

Stage B — Proactive Monitoring with Scheduled Execution

Stage B wraps the Stage A reactive triage in a monitoring loop. The agent runs on a schedule, compares current error patterns against a baseline, and triggers triage automatically when anomalies are detected — without waiting for a human to initiate the process.

Scheduler triggers → Fetch current errors → Compare to baseline	→ Anomaly detected? → Run reactive triage	→ Escalate if needed

Baseline and Anomaly Detection

# monitoring.py — proactive monitoring wrapper
import json, time, os
from datetime import datetime
 
def get_error_baseline() -> dict:
    """Load stored baseline error counts for comparison."""
    # In production: store baseline in HANA or BTP configuration
    return {
        'sm21_hourly_avg': int(os.environ.get('SM21_BASELINE', '10')),
        'sm37_daily_avg': int(os.environ.get('SM37_BASELINE', '5')),
        'anomaly_threshold': 2.0
        # Trigger if errors > 2x baseline
    }
 
def check_for_anomalies(current_counts: dict, baseline: dict) -> dict:
    """Compare current error counts against baseline."""
    sm21_ratio = current_counts.get('sm21_count', 0) / max(baseline['sm21_hourly_avg'], 1)
    sm37_ratio = current_counts.get('sm37_count', 0) / max(baseline['sm37_daily_avg'], 1)
    threshold = baseline['anomaly_threshold']
 
    return {
        'anomaly_detected': sm21_ratio > threshold or sm37_ratio > threshold,
        'sm21_ratio': round(sm21_ratio, 2),
        'sm37_ratio': round(sm37_ratio, 2),
        'trigger_reason': (
            f'SM21 errors at {sm21_ratio:.1f}x baseline' if sm21_ratio > threshold
            else f'SM37 failures at {sm37_ratio:.1f}x baseline'
        ) if sm21_ratio > threshold or sm37_ratio > threshold else None
    }
 
def send_escalation(triage_report: str, targets: list):
    """Send escalation via Microsoft Graph API or Teams webhook."""
    # Integration with MS Graph covered in the training automation article
    # For BTP CF deployment: use requests to call Graph API
    print(f'[{datetime.now()}] Escalation sent to: {targets}')
    print(triage_report[:500])
    # Truncated for logging
 
def run_monitoring_cycle():
    """Single monitoring cycle — run on schedule via CF Scheduler."""
    print(f'[{datetime.now()}] Starting monitoring cycle...')
 
    # Quick error count check — lightweight, runs every cycle
    sm21_data = json.loads(get_sm21_errors.invoke({'hours_back': 1}))
    sm37_data = json.loads(get_sm37_failures.invoke({'hours_back': 24}))
 
    current_counts = {
        'sm21_count': sm21_data.get('count', 0),
        'sm37_count': sm37_data.get('count', 0)
    }
 
    baseline = get_error_baseline()
    anomaly = check_for_anomalies(current_counts, baseline)
 
    if anomaly['anomaly_detected']:
        print(f'Anomaly detected: {anomaly["trigger_reason"]}')
        # Run full reactive triage
        result = run_reactive_triage(hours_back=4)
        # Triage last 4 hours on anomaly
 
        # Escalate if triage confirms high severity
        if 'CRITICAL' in result['triage_report'] or 'HIGH' in result['triage_report']:
            send_escalation(
                result['triage_report'],
                targets=['basis-team@company.com', 'operations@company.com']
            )
    else:
        print(f'No anomaly. SM21: {current_counts["sm21_count"]} errors,
              SM37: {current_counts["sm37_count"]} failures — within baseline.')
 
# BTP CF Flask entry point — triggered by CF Scheduler
from flask import Flask, jsonify
app = Flask(__name__)
 
@app.route('/monitor', methods=['POST'])
def monitor():
    run_monitoring_cycle()
    return jsonify({'status': 'ok', 'timestamp': datetime.now().isoformat()})
 
@app.route('/triage', methods=['POST'])
def triage():
    # Manual reactive triage endpoint
    result = run_reactive_triage(hours_back=24)
    return jsonify(result)

The baseline values (SM21_BASELINE, SM37_BASELINE) are set as BTP environment variables in your manifest.yml. Start with conservative baselines — set them to your current average error counts — and tighten them over time as you observe the agent's sensitivity. Anomaly thresholds that are too low produce alert fatigue. Start at 2x baseline and adjust based on false positive rate.

STAGE A VS STAGE B 

When to Use Each Pattern

Consideration

Stage A — Reactive

Stage B — Proactive

Trigger

Manual or integration call

Scheduled — runs every N minutes

Use case

On-call support, ITSM integration, ad-hoc investigation

Production monitoring, overnight batch, continuous operations

Cost

Runs only when called — lower LLM cost

Runs on schedule — baseline check is lightweight, full triage only on anomaly

Deployment

REST endpoint on BTP CF

CF Scheduler + REST endpoint on BTP CF

Recommended for

Support teams, incident response workflows

Operations teams, 24x7 production environments

NEW PATTERN: ROUTER + SUB-AGENT COORDINATION 

What This Part Introduces for Part 6

Part 3 introduces the router + sub-agent coordination pattern that Part 6 uses to orchestrate all four series agents together. The key principles that carry forward:

  • The router has a global view — it sees all error types and identifies cross-domain correlations that sub-agents cannot see working independently
  • Sub-agents are specialists — they receive focused context from the router and produce higher quality findings than a generalist agent would
  • Context passing is explicit — each sub-agent receives the router's summary as input context, not just raw data
  • Correlation is the router's unique value — the pattern where SM21 infrastructure errors cause SM37 job failures is only visible to the agent with the cross-log view

In Part 6, this same router pattern becomes the orchestrator that delegates to the migration assessment agent from Part 2, the master data quality agent from Part 4, and the S/4HANA readiness agent from Part 5. The triage agent from this part also participates as a sub-agent when the orchestrator detects operational anomalies during assessment runs.

What's Next — Part 4

  • Part 4: SAP Master Data Quality Agent — detects and prioritizes data quality issues across master data domains (material, vendor, customer), generates a scored remediation plan, and integrates with the HANA vector store RAG pipeline from Series 1 for context-enriched findings.
  • Part 5: S/4HANA Readiness Agent — extends the Part 2 migration assessment with clean core analysis, deprecated function module and BAPI detection, and BAdI/enhancement spot mapping for upgrade impact scoring.
  • Part 6: Multi-Agent Orchestration — all four agents coordinated by a unified orchestrator that delegates, synthesizes, and produces a complete SAP landscape intelligence report.

The router + sub-agent pattern from this article is reusable across any SAP domain with multiple related data sources. The same architecture applies to financial audit log analysis, security violation detection across multiple authorization logs, and performance monitoring across work process and database logs.

entry.title: #SITBH2026 🎤 S/4HANA Finance na Prática
entry.content:
Bio: @Marssel700 Marssel Vilaça é instrutor e consultor SAP com atuação em projetos de implementação e transformação digital utilizando SAP S/4HANA. Possui experiência prática no mercado e se dedica à disseminação de conhecimento técnico por meio de conteúdos educacionais e participação ativa na comunidade SAP.

Descrição da Palestra

A palestra apresentada no SAP Inside Track Belo Horizonte 2026 #SITBH2026 aborda experiências reais em projetos SAP S/4HANA, com foco nos principais aprendizados adquiridos durante implementações. O conteúdo traz uma visão prática do que acontece nos bastidores dos projetos, destacando desafios, decisões estratégicas e pontos de atenção para profissionais da área

Resumo do Conteúdo

A apresentação inicia com uma introdução ao contexto do evento SAP Inside Track Belo Horizonte 2026, destacando a importância da comunidade SAP e dos encontros presenciais para troca de conhecimento.

O palestrante contextualiza sua atuação como consultor e instrutor, explicando que o objetivo da palestra é compartilhar experiências reais de projetos com SAP S/4HANA, especialmente para aqueles que não puderam acompanhar o evento presencialmente.

Entre os principais pontos abordados:

A relevância do SAP S/4HANA como plataforma central para transformação digital nas empresas.
A importância de compreender o cenário real dos projetos, indo além da teoria e das documentações oficiais.
A necessidade de preparação adequada dos profissionais SAP para lidar com desafios práticos durante implementações.
O valor da troca de experiências dentro da comunidade, permitindo aprendizado contínuo e evolução profissional.
A apresentação foi originalmente realizada no ambiente do Localiza Labs, reforçando a conexão entre empresas inovadoras e a comunidade SAP.

O conteúdo também enfatiza que cada projeto possui particularidades, exigindo flexibilidade, conhecimento técnico e visão estratégica dos consultores envolvidos.

Considerações Finais

A palestra reforça a importância de compartilhar experiências reais como forma de acelerar o aprendizado coletivo na comunidade SAP. Eventos como o SAP Inside Track Belo Horizonte 2026 se mostram essenciais para conectar profissionais, disseminar conhecimento prático e preparar o mercado para os desafios das implementações com SAP S/4HANA.

entry.title: Re: What makes an employee-churn model usable in the real world?
entry.content:
Just to add from a governance slant, we have heard from customer that embedding governance in systems that are not designed for it is hard!  On the topic of trust, ladies and gentlemen, promote the idea of a data quality analyst, data owner, or data steward for your key data domains - that way you can do your job faster, because you can trust the data! Some thoughts from SAP and non-SAP customers through some recent research:

  • Data environments are often built quickly for analytics and reporting
  • Ownership, documentation, governance controls are underdeveloped; accountability gaps emerge
  • Trust Owners must retrofit governance into fragmented ecosystems with inconsistent definitions/unclear lineage

There is a better way - and so much is automated, but with the human touch and true feels for the business.

Engineers and developers, CTA:  get your data governance started with the people that know the business processes/analytics and the data itself to own that data.  I believe that then you can do your job way more efficiently - and more importantly trust your outcomes. My two cents 😊

entry.title: Unable to Change Posting Date for Physical Inventory in Difference Analyzer - Embedded EWM
entry.content:
Currently it is impossible to set or change the posting date for Physical Inventory Documents. You can define the document date and the counting date, but none of them impact the posting date of the PI document. Whenever we post the difference to ERP side "Analyze Difference" the posting date field is filled with the current date and is unchangeable. 

--- Steps to Reproduce ---

  1. Create a PI document in /SCWM/PI_CREATE
  2. Go to edit mode on PI document and do the "Count" on back date and "Post" the count in EWM and save.
  3. Open the Difference Analyzer app /SCWM/DIFF_ANALYZER
  4. Unable to change the posting date

How to influence the posting date in ERP?


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Unable to Change Posting Date for Physical Inventory...
entry.content:
Hello,

You can use BAdI /SCWM/EX_ERP_GOODSMVT_EXT to influence the posting date on ECC.

Method CHANGE_MATDOC has parameter CS_GOODSMVT_HEADER which has PSTNG_DATE that can be adjusted.

See more information in KBAs 2574109 and 3554439.

Regards,

SAP Technical Support

entry.title: Re: AI Developer Challenge - Week 1: Grounding with SAP Generative AI Hub
Question on Automatic Clearing Rules – Do All Selected Criteria Need to Match?
entry.content:
Hello Experts,

I have a question regarding the Automatic Clearing of Open Items configuration in SAP (transaction OB74).

When defining clearing rules, SAP allows us to specify up to five matching criteria. My understanding is that all selected criteria must match in order for automatic clearing to occur.
Can you please confirm if this is correct?

Additionally, we have a business requirement where different combinations of fields should trigger clearing independently, for example:

  • Clear if Amount + Reference (XBLNR) match
  • Clear if Amount + Billing Document (VBLEN) match
  • Clear if Amount + Assignment (ZUONR) match

Is there a way to configure SAP so that each of these combinations works as an independent clearing rule, instead of requiring all fields in the OB74 configuration to match at the same time?

Any guidance on how to set this up—or whether multiple parallel clearing rules are possible—would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you!

entry.title: CDS View Linkage between Outbound Delivery, ODO, Warehouse Order & Warehouse Task
entry.content:
1. Summary

The blog explains how to establish a complete data linkage between Outbound Delivery (ERP), Outbound Delivery Order (EWM ODO), Warehouse Task (WT), and Warehouse Order (WO) within SAP S/4HANA Cloud, public edition, using standard CDS views.

The key take a way is that although no single CDS view contains all four entities, the full relationship can be achieved by combining standard views through UUID-based joins, without the need for custom fields.

It outlines the full linkage chain:

  • Start from the ERP Outbound Delivery
  • Link to the EWM Outbound Delivery Order via reference document views
  • Connect the ODO to Warehouse Tasks using a bridge view
  • Finally, link Warehouse Tasks directly to Warehouse Orders

The blog also provides detailed join conditions, including the specific CDS views and fields required at each step.

For implementation, it recommends:

  • Creating a custom composite CDS view to consolidate all entities (preferred approach within the VDM extension framework), or
  • Performing sequential queries at application level if CDS extensibility is limited, which can be a constraint in cloud environments like S/4HANA Cloud public edition

Overall, the blog shows that end-to-end traceability across ERP and EWM documents is fully supported using standard SAP data models, even in the public cloud scenario.

2. The Full Linkage Chain

3. Detailed Join Conditions

Link 1: Outbound Delivery (ERP) → Outbound Delivery Order (EWM)

The view I_EWM_OutbDelivOrdHdr already exposes the ERP Delivery Document number via its
_Reference association to I_EWM_DeliveryReferenceDoc_2:

  • I_EWM_OutbDelivOrdHdr.OutboundDelivery — the ERP DN number
  • At item level: I_EWM_OutbDelivOrdItem.OutboundDelivery and OutboundDeliveryItem

The underlying reference document table ( /scdl/db_refdoc ) stores:

  • DeliveryUUID = ODO UUID
  • EWMReferenceDocumentCategory = 'ERP'
  • EWMRefDeliveryDocumentNumber= the ERP Outbound Delivery number

Link 2: Outbound Delivery Order → Warehouse Task

The bridge table /scwm/db_itemwt , exposed as I_EWM_WhseTaskWhseReqItem , provides the link:

Additionally,  I_EWM_WarehouseTask2  itself carries reverse references:

EWMReferenceDocumentCategory — value 'PDO' for outbound delivery orders
ReferenceDocumentUUID — the ODO header UUID
ReferenceDocumentItemUUID — the ODO item UUID

Link 3: Warehouse Task → Warehouse Order

This is a direct field on the Warehouse Task:

  • I_EWM_WarehouseTask_2.WarehouseOrder (from /scwm/ordim_o.who )
  • Joins to I_EWM_WarehouseOrder_2 on EWMWarehouse + WarehouseOrder

4. Key CDS Views and Fields Reference

5. Recommended Approach

Option A: Custom Composite CDS View (Recommended)

Create a custom composite CDS view that joins the standard views. Conceptual SQL:

This is the standard SAP-recommended approach (VDM layer extension) and does not require modifications to any SAP standard objects.

Option B: Application-Level Joins

If it's not possible to create custom CDS views (e.g., cloud restrictions), they can query sequentially:

  1. Query I_EWM_OutbDelivOrdItem to get OutboundDelivery + OutboundDeliveryOrderUUID 
  2. Query I_EWM_WhseTaskWhseReqItem to get WT numbers for each ODO UUID
  3. Query I_EWM_WarehouseTask_2 to get the WarehouseOrder from each WT

entry.title: Blog 4 - Beyond Data Warehousing: AI-assisted migration of Business Logic from BW 7.5 to BDC Part 1
entry.content:
In the earlier parts of this blog series, we discussed the upcoming end‑of‑maintenance deadline (now 2030) for SAP NetWeaver Business Warehouse 7.5 on-premise (SAP BW 7.5 on-prem) customers and the options they have going forward.  

Based on practical experiences in a recent pilot project, we showed how “lifting” an SAP BW system in Private Cloud edition (SAP BW in PCE) in SAP Business Data Cloud (SAP BDC) can extend maintenance of the as-is environment while unlocking new capabilities to kick-start modernization.

This follow on blog explores how SAP BW 7.5 on-premise customers can reuse their existing investments in data extraction and data modeling flows (including complex ABAP transformations and BW business logic) and modernize them by leveraging SAP Datasphere, Business Technology Platform and Business Data Cloud capabilities.

As a recap in the previous blogs, we have seen

  1. the creation of Data Products from SAP BW via ‘Data Product Generator’, that enables SAP BW customers to easily replicate InfoProvider data and its semantics to SAP HANA Cloud data lake files storage (HDLFS) within SAP BDC and transforms traditional BW data into modern data products for consumption in analytical scenarios and AI/ML workflows.
  2. how to integrate BW standard extractors and preserve custom extractors business logic into SAP BDC.

In this part of the blog series, we will focus on how we can leverage existing BW investments on data extraction and transformation (BW DataSources, data models, transformations and ABAP routines, etc) into SAP BDC and leveraging SAP Datasphere and SAP Business Technology Platform capabilities beyond Data Warehousing.

Business Challenges and Objective

One of the main challenges that multiple BW customers face is that in order to transform their business and move into a cloud-native architecture they need to be able to ensure full coverage of their existing BW business logic and data modeling, In addition to that, they need to understand the transition to the cloud with minimal business disruption by leveraging existing investments and custom managed modeling and developments.

Most of the mature BW customers have built for many years complex custom business logic in the daily transformations and data flows in BW that apply specifically to their business. In order to move to the cloud and adopt AI and innovation in data and analytics, they need to ensure seamless migration of their existing business models without manually re-designing the whole business logic in the new environment.

In the example below, we will illustrate how a BW customer can replicate their custom BW business logic written in ABAP to SQL code in SAP Datasphere, in a semi-automated manner.

Custom Example

For our complex BW pilot case, we are using a custom BW transformation that is used to load data from the 2LIS_02_ITM DataSource (Purchase Order Item data) into the custom BW ADSO (advanced DataStore object) including a custom ABAP routine.

First, let’s look at the 2LIS_02_ITM DataSource and source data preview in SAP Business Data Cloud.

Source Data

The below data preview is mainly the data from the remote table in S/4HANA for Purchasing Data (Item Level) (2LIS_02_ITM). More details on how to extract the data in SAP BDC can be found in the previous blog Blog 3 - From BW 7.5 to SAP BDC: Reusing Extractors, Custom Logic, and Models Standard Extractors.

Custom SAP BW Transformation / ABAP Routine

The below example will explain what a custom ABAP routine in SAP BW can do. This is not intended to be a comprehensive tutorial on what an ABAP routine can do in BW, but we believe a summary is useful for the purpose of this blog example.

In our case, the custom ABAP routine in the data transformation is mainly a start routine transferring data from 2LIS_02_ITM data source into a custom-built purchasing DSO.  The custom ABAP routine includes the ABAP code populating the additional object as part of the target DSO.

Example of Custom ABAP routine

To transform the data in Business Data Cloud for the target object we need to migrate the below ABAP routine.

Please note, the following ABAP code is a condensed example intended for demonstration. In our pilot project, the solution was substantially more comprehensive - using exceptions, authorization rules, and extensive customer-specific business logic implemented via enhancements (e.g. user exits) and domain rules.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

AI-assisted replication of BW ABAP Transformation to SQL Code

To replicate complex ABAP transformation with the above example of start routine to Business Data Cloud, SAP Datasphere, we are going to use the GenAI functionality in Generative AI Hub via SAP AI Launchpad by also allowing human in the loop (HITL) to review and improve the AI-assisted generated SQL code. In our case, the main target audience for this role is mainly a Developer with SQL knowledge.

SAP AI Launchpad is a multitenant software as a service (SaaS) application in SAP Business Technology Platform. Customers and partners can use SAP AI Launchpad to manage AI use cases (scenarios) across multiple instances of AI runtimes (such as SAP AI Core). SAP AI Launchpad also provides generative AI capabilities via the Generative AI Hub, that we are showcasing in this example.

In SAP AI Launchpad, navigate to Generative AI Hub → Model Library and select the Claude 4.5 Sonnet model. Alternatively, you can choose from any of the foundation models and versions available in the Model Library based on multiple providers.

 

And ask to create the SQL code for the start routine in ABAP in the Chat menu. 

The prompt mainly is the below:

Please convert below ABAP routine to SQL code for SAP Datasphere SQL view and change only the names of the fields with the equivalent S/4HANA names. Please don’t change the DataSource names in the joins. The source object in SAP Datasphere is not the equivalent CDS view but the 2lis_02_itm DataSource.

The response from GenAI is the below:

 

Now that we have the code generated by GenAI Hub, we can use it in the data builder layer in SAP Datasphere in Business Data Cloud. First, we create a space in SAP Datasphere environment with HANA Database Storage Type:

Then we create a SQL view in SAP Datasphere with the above suggested GenAI code, and we make the below modifications for the source table and joins. Please find below the detailed steps:

  1. Copy the generated code from the AI Launchpad of GenAI Hub. For illustration, this example is limited to an initial overview of the SQL logic and the custom ABAP code used in the start routine. 
--# SAP Datasphere SQL View Conversion for 2LIS_02_ITM DataSource
--Below is the converted SQL code for SAP Datasphere. This transformation uses the **2LIS_02_ITM DataSource** as the source and includes the field mappings to match the actual DataSource field names.
--```sql
==================================================================
-- SAP Datasphere SQL View
-- Converted from ABAP Transformation Routine
-- Source: 2LIS_02_ITM DataSource (Purchasing Document Item)
-- Purpose: Purchase Order Item Data with Vendor Country Enrichment 
--          and Price Calculations
==================================================================
SELECT 
    -- KEY FIELDS (from 2LIS_02_ITM)
==================================================================
    SRC.EBELN AS OI_EBELN,                       -- Purchasing Document Number
    SRC.EBELP AS OI_EBELP,                       -- Item Number of Purchasing Document
==================================================================
    -- ORGANIZATIONAL DATA
==================================================================
    SRC.EKORG AS PURCH_ORG,                      -- Purchasing Organization
.
.
.
. 
    VEN.LAND1 AS COUNTRY -- Enriched field: Vendor Country from Master Data
FROM 
==================================================================
    -- SOURCE: 2LIS_02_ITM DataSource (DO NOT CHANGE)
==================================================================
    "2LIS_02_ITM" AS src==================================================================
    -- VENDOR MASTER DATA JOIN (replaces ABAP custom lookup code)
==================================================================
    LEFT OUTER JOIN "/BI0/PVENDOR" AS VEN
        ON SRC.LIFNR = VEN.VENDOR
        AND VEN.OBJVERS = 'A'                    -- Only active master data records
WHERE 
==================================================================
    -- FILTER CONDITIONS (from routine_9998 - start routine)
    -- OSS Notes: 627498, 636914, 690106
==================================================================
​
  • Create a SQL view in SAP Datasphere with the copied GenAI code.
  • Make the below modifications for the source table and joins.
    1. Replace source table with 2LIS_02_ITM DataSource
    2. Replace SAP BW Vendor master data table with the SAP Datasphere view (based on Data Product Generator object)
    3. Check validity of the code and make any other modifications required (follow GenAI Hub notes and comment out all the non-SQL code or non-existing objects).
    4. Save the SQL view as Fact Semantic Usage and select run in analytical mode and expose for consumption.

 

We can now test the data preview. 

 

These data can now be consumed by the SAP Datasphere Analytic Model layer or can be shared as a custom data product and consumed in the open data Lakehouse architecture environment.

Alternative Solution

Another option for replicating BW business logic written in ABAP to SQL is mainly to leverage SAP Joule for Consultants.

Here below is an example of the same custom ABAP Routine explanation in SAP Joule for Consultants:

SAP Joule for Consultants accelerates SAP projects and cloud transformations with the most authoritative AI assistance, grounded in exclusive SAP knowledge. It enables consultants to increase productivity with fast and reliable answers, minimize rework with expert-level guidance that improves every design decision, and deliver more operational efficiency by ensuring projects align with best practices.

SAP Joule for Consultants provides comprehensive explanation of ABAP code purpose, business logic, and structure, powered by a fine-tuned model trained on the largest and most diverse repository of ABAP code, encompassing 250 million lines of ABAP and 30 million lines of CDS code. Its expert-level guidance is grounded in 9+ terabytes of curated SAP knowledge, including 100+ SAP certifications and 3+ million non-public documents such as SAP Notes and SAP Knowledge Base Articles.

Roadmap

As part of H2.2026 Roadmap, customers can use the query template generator for SAP Business Data Cloud to transfer query metadata from release 7.5 of SAP Business Warehouse (SAP BW) and SAP BW/4HANA to the SAP Datasphere solution within SAP Business Data Cloud. This will mainly mean reducing manual effort needed to transfer a query from SAP BW to SAP Business Data Cloud.

In addition, please stay tuned: a BW Migration tool is planned for SAP Business Data Cloud, that will enable customers to facilitate the migration of BW data flows to SAP BDC.

 

Conclusion

SAP BW customers can now reuse their existing investments from SAP BW 7.5 on HANA and leverage the modern capabilities of Business Data Cloud to consume and analyze the BW data in future proof scalable architecture without the need to re-design or rebuilt everything from scratch.

 

Please stay tuned for the 5th blog post in this series, ‘Blog 5 - Beyond Data Warehousing, AI-assisted migration of Business Logic from BW 7.5 to BDC Part 2’.

entry.title: SuccessFactors Business Rules: Now it's time for a deep dive in the format() function!
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

I want to share with you some tips around the format function. It’s one of the most important functions used in Business Rules, as it allows you to combine different elements into meaningful text. For example, you can use it to merge a country code (e.g., DE), a business unit name (e.g., SMB), and a number (e.g., 111) into a single value like DESMB111.

The format function is also useful for tasks such as concatenation, adding leading zeros to codes, formatting dates, and more. For a deeper dive, check out the Implementing Business Rules in SAP SuccessFactors document in the Help Portal.

Here are some examples: 

Simple Format: Let’s create a basic template. In this example, we are generating a position code:

  • % → start of the formatter
  • d → conversion digit

Upper Letter format [Strings: %s vs %S]: Some customers would like to store the first and last name information in uppercase.

As you can see above, the function has the following behavior:

  • %s → prints the string as-is
  • %S → converts the entire string to uppercase

Different Arguments [%N$] : This function can also be used to concatenate information from different fields. I personally prefer using it because it makes it easy to include elements like spaces between words.

  • %1$s → first argument (First Name)
  • %2$s → second argument (Last Name)

Adding leading zeros: In some cases, it’s necessary to add leading zeros to an external code. For example, a position code might need to be 8 digits long and generated sequentially.

  • 08 → represents the max number of digits that should be considered

Substring: This function can also be used to extract a substring, such as when defaulting the initials.

  • .1 → represents the number of chars that will be considered from the text

Rounding: The format function can also be used to display a decimal value with a specific number of decimal places.

  • .2 → represents the number of decimal places that should be considered from the calculation
  • f → floating type

Date/Time formatting: You can also use the function to format information based on date and time.

In terms of date/time formats here is a small list of the most used formatters:

FormatterDescriptionExample

%tY

Year (4 digits)

2026

%ty

Year (2 digits)

26

%tm

Month (01–12)

04

%td

Day (01–31)

06

%tB

Full month name

April

%tb

Short month name

Apr

%tA

Full weekday

Monday

%ta

Short weekday

Mon

%tH

Hour (00–23)

14

%tI

Hour (01–12)

02

%tM

Minute

35

%tS

Seconds

20

%tp

am/pm

pm

%tF

ISO date

2026-04-06

%tD

US date

04/06/26

%tc

Full date/time

Mon Apr 06 14:35:20 UTC 2026

%tr

12-hour time

02:35:20 PM

%tT

24-hour time

14:35:20

I hope this information has been helpful and encourages you to explore our standard functions to streamline your own implementation projects! As always, please discuss with your customers the pros and cons of adopting such a function in your projects.

entry.title: S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition (002) - OSの最新セキュリティパッチ適用
entry.content:
OSに最新のセキュリティパッチを適用し、システムの安全性を維持することは、システム運用において重要なタスクの一つです。

本記事では、SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Editionをご利用の際に、OSの最新セキュリティパッチ適用を依頼する方法をご紹介します。

 

未適用のOSセキュリティパッチの確認

 

SAP for Meへログイン後、以下の手順で確認できます。
「Services & Support」→「Private Cloud Workspace」→「Risk Management」セクションの「Security Patches」→「Operating System」タブを選択し、対象のSIDをクリックしてください。

また、以下のURLへアクセスすることで、適用が必要なセキュリティパッチを確認することも可能です。
URL: https://me.sap.com/privcloudsecuritypatches#?variant=standard 

下記は、サンプル画面となります。

 

利用するSRテンプレート

 

OSのセキュリティパッチ適用を依頼する際は、以下のSRテンプレートを使用します。
SRテンプレート：Apply latest Security Patch to OS (Linux, Windows)

下記は、本記事作成時点におけるSR作成画面です。

 

Creating with Advance Scheduling：NO　※特別な理由がない限り、デフォルト値のままご使用ください。

Set system messages(for ABAP systems): ABAPシステムにおいて、メンテナンス期間中に表示するシステムメッセージを設定するかどうかを指定する項目です。


注意事項

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Editionでは、以下のように複数のシステムコンポーネントが提供されています。

  • SAP Cloud Connector
  • Web Dispatcher
  • S/4HANA（AP Server + SAP HANA）
    など

SAP HANAのSIDを選択してSRを作成した場合、AP ServerおよびSAP HANAのOSに対してセキュリティパッチが適用されます。
一方で、SAP Cloud ConnectorおよびWeb Dispatcherについては、それぞれのSIDごとにSRを作成する必要があります。


まとめ


システムを脆弱性から保護するため、OSのセキュリティパッチの定期的な適用を推奨いたします。
なお、適用作業にはダウンタイムが伴うため、事前にメンテナンス時間を確保した上で、計画的に実施することをご検討ください。

entry.title: Re: How to Get a View of Supplier Payments and Customer C...
entry.content:
Dear @Jessicaaguilar1,

The standard functionality for this requirement is Cash Flow Analyzer with drilldown to Check Cash Flow Items. SAP documents that Cash Flow Analyzer supports analysis over days, weeks, months, quarters or years and that you can navigate to Check Cash Flow Items for the underlying line-item details. SAP also documents that One Exposure from Operations derives forecasted cash flows for receivables and payables based on the net due date or cash discount date

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/186460fdc35a4b64a713da9bb00deb1e/573ee5f7021f4f6ee10000000a42189c.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/186460fdc35a4b64a713da9bb00deb1e/cd6705281eef4230b81ef5a7b1ec54ee.html

The limitation is that SAP delivers this as a combined liquidity forecast with drilldown, not as a separate predefined AP/AR weekly matrix with a custom side-by-side layout for supplier invoices and customer invoices, however there is a supported workaround. SAP documents released CDS content for Cash and Liquidity Management, including Cash Position and Liquidity Forecast Query (C_LiquidityPositionQuery) and Cash Flow Query. If you needs a specific weekly presentation beyond the standard app layout the defensible SAP-standard approach is to build the report on top of this released cash/liquidity analytical content rather than look for another standard operational app

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/186460fdc35a4b64a713da9bb00deb1e/be968afbde79451abb255fe123b3a17b.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/c0c54048d35849128be8e872df5bea6d/17a419741df942cda71c2b1689c1b8f5.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/c0c54048d35849128be8e872df5bea6d/05084020890140aab5bba044dd5491e6.html

If SAP Analytics Cloud is in scope, SAP also documents Liquidity Planning with SAP Analytics Cloud based on integrated SAP S/4HANA Cloud data sources. That is the stronger SAP-standard option when the requirement is not only to review the standard forecast but also to present it in a more tailored planning/reporting format

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/186460fdc35a4b64a713da9bb00deb1e/39e81b8f567d415185768de918b5092d.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_ANALYTICS_CLOUD/42093f14b43c485fbe3adbbe81eff6c8/b834ee07a87042c1a163af1869472dd8.html

Summary:

The standard app is Cash Flow Analyzer + Check Cash Flow Items. If the customer needs the same data in a stricter weekly report layout, the supported workaround is custom analytics on the released cash/liquidity CDS query content and optionally SAP Analytics Cloud if that is part of the solution scope

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: CLEAR ITAB or REFRESH ITAB
entry.content:
Dear @Dipeshhhh21,

In modern ABAP, CLEAR itab is the recommended statement and REFRESH itab should not be used in new code. SAP documents that REFRESH itab is obsolete and that for an internal table without a header line, REFRESH itab has the same effect as CLEAR itab more generally, SAP describes REFRESH itab as equivalent to CLEAR itab. SAP also states that tables with header lines are obsolete and forbidden in classes, which is why using REFRESH instead of CLEAR is obsolete as well

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/doc/abapdocu_752_index_htm/7.52/en-US/abaprefresh_itab.htm

https://help.sap.com/doc/abapdocu_cp_index_htm/CLOUD/en-US/ABAPCLEAR.html

So:

  • CLEAR itab: deletes all rows of the internal table and keeps the initial memory requirement reserved. SAP explicitly notes this can even be beneficial when you will fill the table again

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/doc/abapdocu_cp_index_htm/CLOUD/en-US/ABAPCLEAR.html

  • REFRESH itab: for normal modern internal tables, effectively the same as CLEAR itab but SAP classifies it as obsolete syntax
  • FREE itab: deletes all rows and releases the complete memory area used by the rows, including the initial memory requirement. SAP positions this for cases where memory really should be released as much as possible

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/doc/abapdocu_latest_index_htm/latest/en-US/ABAPFREE_DATAOBJECT.html

Summary:

CLEAR itab is better in modern ABAP, not because it is functionally stronger than REFRESH but because it is the current, non-obsolete, standard statement. REFRESH gives no advantage in modern code. If memory release is the real requirement then the better statement is not REFRESH but FREE itab

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: POD confirmation needed after billing due to Carta Porte process - how to handle VLPOD? - Mexico
entry.content:
Hello SAP experts,

I would like to ask for guidance regarding a process design issue in SAP SD / LE for Mexico.

In the standard SAP process, Proof of Delivery (POD) with transaction VLPOD is normally used before billing, since the confirmed POD quantity can become the basis for invoicing.

However, in Mexico we have a legal/fiscal process constraint:

  • First, we need to create the billing document
  • Then we generate the CFDI / Carta Porte complement
  • Only after that can the transportation legally leave
  • Finally, once the customer actually receives the goods, we can confirm the delivery

Because of this sequence, the standard POD logic does not fit well, since the billing must already exist before the physical delivery is confirmed.

Our question is:

How can this scenario be handled in SAP when POD confirmation is required only after billing has already been created?

More specifically, we would like to understand:

  1. Is there any standard SAP approach to allow POD confirmation after billing?
  2. Can VLPOD still be used in some way only as a delivery confirmation step, without affecting the billing basis?
  3. Has anyone implemented this scenario in Mexico, especially where billing is mandatory first in order to generate Carta Porte?

We are trying to find the best design that:

  • complies with the Mexican legal requirement,
  • allows billing before transportation,
  • and still lets us confirm the actual delivery afterwards.

Any recommendations regarding standard configuration, best practice, or enhancement approach would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you in advance.

entry.title: Re: Sustainability Control Tower for BRSR - India
entry.content:
Hi Narendra, I hope you're doing great.

Great question! SAP Sustainability Control Tower (SCT) does support the BRSR (Business Responsibility & Sustainability Reporting) framework through its Qualitative Metrics feature, available since the March 2025 release.

Using the Manage Metrics application, you can create custom qualitative metrics aligned with BRSR by defining:

  • Reporting Standard → BRSR

  • Disclosure Requirement → specific Section and Principle (e.g., Section-C, Principle-2)

  • Data Point → Essential or Leadership Indicators

  • Reference Type → Narrative, Semi-Narrative, or Boolean

Once metrics are created, data can be maintained via the Manage Qualitative ESG Data application.

Please note that SAP does not currently provide out-of-the-box BRSR content (SAP-provided qualitative metrics are ESRS-aligned). However, the platform's extensibility fully supports BRSR requirements through custom configuration.

For a detailed step-by-step walkthrough covering both ESRS and BRSR framework setup in SCT — including Sustainability Matter mapping, Disclosure References, and Data Points specific to BRSR — I'd recommend the following SAP Community blog, which covers exactly your use case:

Managing Qualitative Metrics in SAP Sustainability Control Tower (SCT) for ESRS and BRSR Framework — by Pardhasaradhi Reddy Chelikam

Hope this helps!

entry.title: Valuated Assembly Item Usage Effects on CO FI
entry.content:
Dear Experts,

I'm trying to implement a assembly processing to my customer where they are a buy and sell company but sometimes they need to assemble some of materials that they have procured and deliver them. Which is a perfect enviroment from my knowledge where they also want to implement Project Systems and they don't want to implement Production Planning module. I have gone through the process, but i have one topic that im stuck with which is the relationship or maybe the best word is balance between the;

- GR Material's value or maybe COGS value in another word (Please correct me if it can differ)
-  Raw Material Consumption + Activity Confirmations


The GR Material's cost is written a credit to the Network Activity but as far as i know since I'm a PS consultant not a FICO consultant, these values needs to be settled to the Material at the period end once GR has been done if material is Standart Price Material which most of them my customer wants them to be.

Since I cant settle the costs from Network Activity, Network or WBS Element to Material directly, how should i be implementing this functionality for Semi-Finished or Finished goods with Standart Price where i dont break the system at the end for Controlling and Financial side?

Am I missing something where actually a Settlement Rule to Material can be auto generated? I have tried TECO after Final Confirmation, nothing happened. I have even tried to create settlement rules from BAPI for Material which CJ88 just doesn't settle anyway.

Or was this process was designed as these semi-finished or Finished goods should be implemented as Moving Avarage Price. 

Or Is there another period end allocation method where we should go to rather then directly settling everything to Material?

I would really appreciate the support on this matter since I have been doing an R&D on this topic for so long and quite stuck on this topic. I have putted an example screenshot of the network activity after the GR which i believe should be settled to Material.

Thanks And Regards,
Mert.

entry.title: Re: Unable to adapt standard Fiori Travel Request app – m...
entry.content:
Hello.

For adaptation or adaptability, I typically check the Fiori Launchpad Library to see if the app can support extensibility.  The Adapt UI only supports what the Fiori app can provide.

So with this said, I will rely on the main manual of the Travel Request app.

Assuming your My Request App is this Fiori App ID 

App ID
F0409 per URL Link:  https://fioriappslibrary.hana.ondemand.com/sap/fix/externalViewer/#/detail/Apps('F0409')/S20OP 

then their main manual is:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_ON-PREMISE/f4153779fcb742c094bb6157c0ff1cbe/c7a57f583c630a02e10000000a44147b.html?version=2020.002 

From an Adapt UI perspective, another way to check is to find the manifest.json of the actual Fiori app.  in that manifest.json see if the parameter isAdaptationAllowed = true - you may search that too in the Network HAR trace or search for that keyword: isAdaptationAllowed. But again, not all Fiori apps support adaptation and the team cannot simply assumed all FIori apps support it.
So from our perspective, double check the app documentation.  If it says you can do it, you can do it.  Otherwise, most likely you can assumed you can't.

Regards,
Mark
